summary: Cantrill argues that Oracle's decision to stop contributing to OpenSolaris is strategically foolish, comparing it to L. Paul Bremer's disbanding of the Iraqi military—a move that needlessly created adversaries from potential allies. He clarifies that Oracle hasn't closed the project but rather forked it, betting they can out-innovate the community at precisely the moment when Solaris talent is dispersing. Paradoxically, this abandonment has liberated companies like Joyent to take full control of their technical destiny and innovate freely.
key_points: Oracle's decision to stop contributing to OpenSolaris has effectively created unnecessary adversaries from the project's strongest potential allies
One cannot close an open source project—one can only fork it, so Oracle has chosen to compete with rather than cooperate with the community
The departure of talented engineers like Adam Leventhal represents an irreversible breaking of Oracle's monopoly on Solaris expertise
Certainty of Oracle's non-engagement actually liberates dependent businesses to invest and plan accordingly
Joyent engineers are now conceiving innovations they wouldn't have considered when Oracle's involvement was uncertain
money_quotes: As it apparently needs to be said, one cannot close an open source project – one can only fork it.
Beyond merely a foolish decision borne out of a distorted worldview, it has created combatants unnecessarily.
One does not replace an engineer like Adam; one can only hope to grow another.
Time and time again, I saw Adam not only come up with innovative solutions to tough problems, but run those innovations through the punishing gauntlet that separates idea from product.
Instead of waiting for Oracle to engage the community, we can be secure in the knowledge that no engagement is forthcoming – and we can invest and plan accordingly.
Yes, hard problems lie ahead – but ideas are flowing, and the future feels alive with possibility; in short, innovation is afoot!
key_insight: Corporate abandonment of an open source project can paradoxically liberate its community by replacing uncertain dependency with the freedom to fully own their technical destiny.
